physical-ai.whyExists: The term exists because language and image models do not move boxes, navigate warehouses, or survive factory floors by themselves. Embodied systems need perception, control, planning, simulation, and safety engineering tied to physical constraints.

askNext: Are they borrowing his execution style, or just his mythology?

pieter-levels.body: The useful lesson is not that every company should be one person. It is that many companies became inefficient long before AI gave anyone a sharper mirror.
pieter-levels.example: "The moment someone invoked @levelsio, the roadmap became less a plan than an accusation that the company might simply have too many meetings."
pieter-levels.misuse: By turning an unusual operator with an audience into a universal organisational theory. His style is instructive, but it does not erase survivorship bias, domain complexity, support work, or the awkward truth that most founders are not Pieter Levels with a timeline full of customers.
pieter-levels.note: His own site uses the name Pieter Levels, even though people online often flatten it to Peter Levels. The distinction is minor; the mythology is not.
pieter-levels.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Levels usually means solo-founder leverage, fast iteration, blunt market testing, and a belief that AI tools amplify people who already know how to ship, write, and sell.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: An uncomfortable reminder that speed, taste, and an audience can outcompete a small committee.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped symbol for solo-builder leverage in the age of AI-assisted product work.

pieter-levels.whyExists: Because some names stop referring only to a person and start referring to a method. Invoking Levels usually means shipping in public, bootstrapping, keeping teams tiny, and treating software as something to release before the deck is finished apologising.

pilot-purgatory.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means many vendor meetings, a few polished demos, no durable workflow change, and a graveyard of licences nobody remembers approving.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

pilot-purgatory.whyExists: Because demos are easier than deployment, sponsors like novelty more than process change, and real adoption usually requires data access, security review, staff enablement, governance, and someone to own the outcome.

post-training.practicalMeaning: In practice it means expensive data curation, human or synthetic preference signals, eval loops, reward design, refusal policies, task-specific tuning, and long arguments about whether capability gains came from the base model or the scaffolding layered on top.

post-training.whyExists: Because a pretrained model may have broad knowledge yet still be poor at following instructions, using tools, refusing unsafe requests, formatting outputs, or behaving in ways customers and policy teams can live with.

project-glasswing.body: The important shift is not the partner list. It is that capability is now being framed as something the industry must manage before it fully commodifies.
project-glasswing.example: "Project Glasswing was the moment the industry stopped speaking about coding gains in the abstract and started sounding like it had met a regulator."

project-glasswing.note: Project Glasswing is an initiative, not a model. The model inside the story is Claude Mythos Preview.

translations.text: A controlled early-access lane for a model that made the cyber conversation feel less theoretical.

project-glasswing.warningLabel: Restricted access is a control, not a theory of the future.
project-glasswing.whyExists: Models have become strong enough at code and systems reasoning that cybersecurity is no longer just another benchmark story. If a model can inspect, understand, and alter complex software unusually well, defence and offence both start moving faster.

askNext: What exact context is being sent to the model?
Which parts of the prompt are fixed system behaviour versus user input?
Is the prompt compensating for a missing tool, missing retrieval step, or missing product rule?
prompt.body: The important operational question is rarely "what prompt did you type?" It is "what exactly did the system send?" Those are not always the same thing.
prompt.example: "The issue was not that the model was weak; the prompt was half policy manual, half legal disclaimer, and no longer resembled a usable instruction."
prompt.misuse: It gets treated either as wizardry or as trivia. Both are wrong. Prompt quality matters, but prompting is not a substitute for missing product design, missing data, or missing control logic. A good prompt cannot rescue a bad system forever.
prompt.note: In image generation, "prompt" often refers to descriptive text steering the style or content of an output. In LLM products, it usually refers to the full assembled input context, not just the visible user message.
prompt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the assembled context sent to the model: system instructions, user text, retrieved snippets, examples, tool schemas, output rules, and maybe the remnants of last week's prompt experiments still haunting production.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A carefully packed bag of instructions, examples, constraints, and context, all fighting for the model's attention.

prompt.whyExists: The term exists because the way you frame input materially affects output. Language models do not simply receive a question. They respond to the entire text context they are given.

askNext: Which parts of the prompt are fixed policy, and which parts are task-specific assembly?
Where do retrieved context, examples, and tool outputs enter the stack?
What happens when you remove one section: collapse, improvement, or merely a shorter superstition?
prompt-architecture.body: Prompt architecture is useful when it names structure rather than swagger. If the system still consists of one swollen string and a prayer, the architecture remains aspirational.
prompt-architecture.example: "Their prompt architecture improved the moment they stopped letting every new requirement attach itself to the same immortal system message."
prompt-architecture.misuse: It gets used as a grand title for very long prompts and very little discipline. Sometimes the work really is architectural. Sometimes the phrase is just a more respectable way of saying nobody wants to untangle the string anymore.
prompt-architecture.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating stable policy from task-level instructions, defining insertion points for retrieval and tool results, deciding what gets versioned, and preventing one helpful addition after another from turning the whole prompt into legal compost.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the prompt stack so the model receives fewer contradictions and the team receives fewer mysteries.

prompt-architecture.whyExists: It exists because complex AI systems no longer send one neat instruction and hope for the best. They assemble multiple layers of context, and the order, boundaries, and ownership of those layers affect reliability more than anyone wanted to admit in the early prompt-engineering era.

askNext: Which part of the prompt is actually stable enough to be reused?
How long do cache hits survive on this provider before they quietly disappear?
Are you measuring real latency and cost gains, or just admiring the idea of efficiency?
prompt-caching.body: Prompt caching is one of those useful terms that sounds more glamorous than it is. The glamour fades the moment someone randomises the first thousand tokens and wonders why the savings have left the building.
prompt-caching.example: "Prompt caching finally started paying off once they stopped putting the customer-specific chaos before the reusable instructions."
prompt-caching.misuse: It gets pitched as free speed or magical memory. In reality it only helps when prompts share the right prefix, the provider's cache keeps it warm, and the traffic pattern is disciplined enough to hit it again before it expires.
prompt-caching.note: The cache is not your application's memory. It is an opportunistic discount for repeated prompt structure.
prompt-caching.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means putting stable instructions first, volatile user data later, watching cache hit rates, and discovering that prompt order has quietly become a finance decision.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Reuse the expensive beginning of a prompt so the invoice and the wait both shrink a little.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A provider-side shortcut that rewards repeated prompt prefixes and punishes sloppy prompt layout.

prompt-caching.whyExists: It exists because long instructions, tool schemas, examples, and reference context are expensive to process from scratch every time. If large parts of a prompt repeat, caching lets systems avoid doing the same preparatory work over and over.

askNext: Who owns this prompt, and what tests prove each instruction still matters?

prompt-debt.body: Prompt debt matters because prompts are now product logic by other means. Treating them as disposable notes is a good way to acquire a permanent archaeology project.
prompt-debt.example: "The prompt looked comprehensive until the fifth exception contradicted the second principle and the model began choosing its favourite manager."
prompt-debt.misuse: It gets romanticised as artisanal prompt craft or dismissed as not real engineering because it lives in text. In truth, prompt debt is ordinary technical debt wearing conversational clothing.
prompt-debt.practicalMeaning: In practice it means contradictory instructions, duplicated examples, stale references, unexplained magic phrases, harder debugging, slower onboarding, and a model that obeys yesterday's emergency patch long after everyone forgets why it was invited.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: A text-shaped pile of shortcuts that will eventually demand architecture.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The prompt still works, but nobody can explain why without opening four old incident documents.

prompt-debt.whyExists: Prompting is fast, legible, and dangerously hospitable to tactical fixes. Teams keep adding caveats, examples, and exceptions because it works now, while the eventual complexity invoice waits with unusual patience.

askNext: Where is the firewall placed: before retrieval, after retrieval, before tools, after tools, or merely in the sales deck?

prompt-firewall.body: The prompt firewall is most convincing when it produces boring evidence: what it blocked, what it missed, and what the system still refuses to trust it with.
prompt-firewall.example: "The prompt firewall caught the obvious injection and missed the support document that asked the agent to email payroll with theatrical confidence."
prompt-firewall.misuse: It gets sold as a complete shield. A firewall can reduce known classes of risk, but it cannot replace threat modelling, least-privilege tools, red-team testing, or the habit of not handing production powers to a chatbot with a clipboard.
prompt-firewall.practicalMeaning: In practice it means classifiers, rules, allowlists, deny lists, logging, human review paths, and careful placement around retrieval and tool execution. The hard part is blocking the right things without turning the product into a polite refusal machine.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Inspect AI traffic for risky instructions, leakage, and tool abuse.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful guardrail that becomes dangerous when everyone starts treating it as the whole fence.

prompt-firewall.warningLabel: A control layer, not an absolution layer.
prompt-firewall.whyExists: AI systems accept natural language from users, documents, tools, and websites. That makes ordinary application boundaries porous, so teams need checks that understand text, intent, context, and the strange confidence of a poisoned instruction.

prompt-injection.practicalMeaning: In practice it means any tool-using or retrieval-heavy system must assume that documents, web pages, emails, and user content may contain adversarial instructions that need filtering, isolation, or refusal.

askNext: Which provider differences are truly hidden, and which still leak into the product?
What breaks when you switch models: quality, tool behaviour, latency, or compliance?
Are you abstracting the transport, the feature set, or the user experience?
provider-abstraction.body: Provider abstraction is often sensible engineering. It becomes theology when teams speak as if a shared interface has abolished the underlying differences that still decide cost, quality, and risk.
provider-abstraction.example: "Their provider abstraction made it easy to switch endpoints and slightly less easy to explain why the outputs changed character the moment finance wanted a cheaper option."
provider-abstraction.misuse: It gets sold as effortless interchangeability. In reality, you can abstract the API more easily than you can abstract behaviour, tool support, latency patterns, safety systems, or the awkward fact that different models fail in different accents.
provider-abstraction.note: A provider abstraction can reduce switching cost and still fail to make providers meaningfully equivalent.
provider-abstraction.practicalMeaning: In practice it means adapter layers, schema translation, feature fallbacks, observability, routing policy, and constant arguments over whether to target the least common denominator or preserve the parts each provider is unusually good at.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: One interface over several providers, plus a fresh category of mismatch tickets.

provider-abstraction.warningLabel: Portability usually stops where model behaviour starts.
provider-abstraction.whyExists: It exists because providers differ in APIs, pricing, uptime, rate limits, model access, and feature support, while product teams would still like to behave as though swapping them were a sign of maturity rather than a fresh integration programme.
